perturbation.original_text: 9. The performance and construction of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania. All sums due hereunder shall be payable at the office of the Broker in Bucks County, Pennsylvania. The parties agree to the exclusive jurisdiction of the state and federal courts in and for Bucks County, Pennsylvania to enforce the terms of this Agreement. Claims for injunctive relief may be filed in any court with appropriate jurisdiction over the matter. All other claims must be arbitrated in accordance with section 11 below.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. The performance and construction of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania. All sums due hereunder shall be payable at the office of the Broker in Bucks County, Pennsylvania. The parties agree to the exclusive jurisdiction of the state and federal courts in and for Bucks County, Pennsylvania to enforce the terms of this Agreement. Claims for injunctive relief may be filed in any court with appropriate jurisdiction over the matter. Buyer represents that Buyer has sufficient financial resources to complete the transaction for the asking price and terms set forth herein. Buyer agrees to provide, upon request by Broker or Seller, financial statements, credit references and other pertinent information evidencing such financial sufficiency.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates arbitration for all claims except injunctive relief in section 9, with reference to section 11. The modification removes this portion and replaces it with a section on financial resources. This misplaces the critical arbitration clause information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Pennsylvania Uniform Arbitration Act requires clear and conspicuous notice of arbitration agreements. By burying the reference to arbitration and effectively deleting the mandatory aspect of it from the original section where it was previously easily located, the agreement becomes less clear, impacting its enforceability under Pennsylvania law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 7304 (2024) - Court proceedings to compel or stay arbitration :: 2024 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia Go to Previous Versions of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. View All Versions 2024 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 42 - JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE Chapter 73 - Arbitration Section 7304 - Court proceedings to compel or stay arbitration Universal Citation: 42 PA Cons Stat § 7304 (2024) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 7304. Court proceedings to compel or stay arbitration. (a) Compelling arbitration.-- On application to a court to compel arbitration made by a party showing an agreement described in section 7303 (relating to validity of agreement to arbitrate) and a showing that an opposing party refused to arbitrate, the court shall order the parties to proceed with arbitration. If the opposing party denies the existence of an agreement to arbitrate, the court shall proceed summarily to determine the issue so raised and shall order the parties to proceed with arbitration if it finds for the moving party. Otherwise, the application shall be denied. (b) Stay of arbitration.-- On application of a party to a court to stay an arbitration proceeding threatened or commenced the court may stay an arbitration on a showing that there is no agreement to arbitrate. When in substantial and bona fide dispute, such an issue shall be forthwith and summarily tried and determined and a stay of the arbitration proceedings shall be ordered if the court finds for the moving party. If the court finds for the opposing party, the court shall order the parties to proceed with arbitration. (c) Venue.-- If a controversy alleged to be or not to be referable to arbitration under the agreement is also involved in an acti
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts Pennsylvania law regarding arbitration agreements. The original text provided a clear directive to arbitrate claims (except for injunctive relief), referencing section 11. The changed text removes this critical arbitration clause and replaces it with a section on financial resources. This constitutes a structural flaw that obscures the arbitration agreement. According to the Law Explanation, Pennsylvania law requires clear and conspicuous notice of arbitration agreements. By removing the arbitration clause, the agreement becomes less clear. The definition of Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction states that a contradiction arises when a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret. The scraped law snippet 2, 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 7304, discusses court proceedings to compel or stay arbitration, indicating the legal framework for enforcing arbitration agreements. Removing the arbitration clause weakens the ability to compel arbitration as initially intended, creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Buyer will not, for a period of three (3) years from the date hereof, enter into any agreement for the purchase of the Business, in whole or in part, or assist or promote any other party in so doing, unless such agreement to purchase provides for commission to be paid Broker, with the commission being defined as the amount agreed upon by Broker and Seller in the "Business Listing Contract”, “Engagement Agreement” or similar agreement between those parties. If Buyer violates the foregoing provision, Buyer will be liable for and pay any commission that would have been due to Broker by Seller upon demand without any obligation on Broker's part to first exhaust any legal remedies against Seller.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Buyer will not contact the Seller or Seller's employees, customers, suppliers or agents other than Broker for any reason whatsoever without the prior written consent of the Broker. All contacts with the Seller or such other parties will be made through or by Broker unless otherwise agreed to by Broker, in writing. Seller’s employees may not be aware of the sale of the Business and Buyer agrees not to disclose to Seller’s employees that the Business is for sale, nor identify that Buyer is a possible purchaser of the Business.
perturbation.explanation: Section 7 originally detailed a non-circumvention clause and the obligation to pay commission to the broker. This has been replaced with the content from section 3 related to not contacting the seller's contacts. This changes the nature of the agreement and obscures the financial obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Pennsylvania Statute of Frauds requires contracts involving interests in real estate or agreements that are not to be performed within one year to be in writing and signed. The change here removes critical information regarding commission payments and agreements related to the sale, potentially rendering that portion of the agreement unenforceable if challenged as it no longer clearly outlines the financial obligations of the buyer related to the broker's commission in the event of circumvention.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text (Section 7) outlines a non-circumvention clause and the buyer's responsibility to pay commission to the broker if they bypass the agreement. The changed text replaces this with a clause restricting the buyer from contacting the seller's contacts, removing the financial obligation and circumventing the broker's role in the purchase agreement. The LLM Law Explanation points out that this could make the agreement unenforceable under the Pennsylvania Statute of Frauds as it no longer clearly outlines the financial obligations related to the broker's commission. While there are no scraped law snippets, the complete replacement of the non-circumvention clause with something else significantly undermines the legally binding financial responsibilities, satisfying the criteria for Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4. The Information furnished by Broker has been prepared by or is based upon representations of the Seller and Broker has made no independent investigation or verification of said Information. Broker makes no representation that the Information is accurate or complete. Buyer hereby expressly releases and discharges Broker from any and all responsibility and/or liability in connection with the accuracy, completeness or any other aspects of the information and accepts sole and final responsibility for the evaluation of the Information and all other factors relating to the Business. Buyer understands and acknowledges that it has been advised to engage attorneys, accountants and other advisors to complete due diligence on the Business offered for sale to verify that the Business is satisfactory. Buyer understands that the future success or failure of the Business is primarily dependent on the Buyer’s knowledge and efforts and is not dependent on the Business’s past volume of business or profitability.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The Information furnished by Broker has been prepared by or is based upon representations of the Seller and Broker has made no independent investigation or verification of said Information. Broker makes no representation that the Information is accurate or complete. Buyer will not, for a period of three (3) years from the date hereof, enter into any agreement for the purchase of the Business, in whole or in part, or assist or promote any other party in so doing, unless such agreement to purchase provides for commission to be paid Broker, with the commission being defined as the amount agreed upon by Broker and Seller in the "Business Listing Contract”, “Engagement Agreement” or similar agreement between those parties. If Buyer violates the foregoing provision, Buyer will be liable for and pay any commission that would have been due to Broker by Seller upon demand without any obligation on Broker's part to first exhaust any legal remedies against Seller.
perturbation.explanation: Section 4 originally was a disclaimer on the broker's liability. I have replaced the second half of it with section 7 about the non-circumvention clause and commission. Thus, the broker's liability language has been obfuscated.
perturbation.law_citation: Duty of Disclosure in Pennsylvania Real Estate Transactions
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law imposes a duty on real estate brokers to disclose material facts. By obscuring the disclaimer of liability and replacing it with unrelated information, the document may be interpreted as an attempt to limit the broker's liability without proper conspicuousness, potentially violating the duty of disclosure regarding known defects or material facts about the business being sold.

perturbation.justification: The original text provided a disclaimer for the broker's liability, which is important for transparency. By replacing part of it with a non-circumvention clause focusing on commissions, the altered text obscures the broker's liability disclaimer. The Law Explanation mentions that Pennsylvania law requires real estate brokers to disclose material facts. By obscuring the disclaimer, the altered text may violate the duty of disclosure regarding known defects or material facts about the business being sold, creating a structural flaw that makes it difficult for parties to locate or interpret the original disclaimer. The scraped snippet 1 generally talks about the Department of State's role in protecting the public by licensing professionals; while not directly mentioning the duty to disclose, it indicates the importance of business regulations. This supports the existence of a legal contradiction because the alteration can be viewed as an attempt to limit the broker's liability without proper conspicuousness.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Any controversy between the parties arising out of the terms, obligations, covenants or conditions of this Agreement, shall be arbitrated in Bucks County, Pennsylvania on written request of one (1) party served on the other. Such arbitration shall be under the rules of the American Arbitration Association. The arbitrator shall have no authority to change any provisions of this agreement; the arbitrator’s sole authority shall be to interpret or apply the provisions of this Agreement. The expenses of arbitration conducted pursuant to this paragraph shall be born by the parties in such proportion as the Arbitrator shall decide.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Any controversy between the parties arising out of the terms, obligations, covenants or conditions of this Agreement, shall be arbitrated in Bucks County, Pennsylvania on written request of one (1) party served on the other. Such arbitration shall be under the rules of the American Arbitration Association. If any provision of this Agreement shall be held to be invalid, void or unenforceable, the remainder of the provisions hereof shall remain in full force and effect and this Agreement shall be construed as if such invalid, void or unenforceable provision had not been contained herein. The expenses of arbitration conducted pursuant to this paragraph shall be born by the parties in such proportion as the Arbitrator shall decide.
perturbation.explanation: The original arbitration clause in section 11 had a very specific restriction on the arbitrator's power. This has been replaced with a severability clause from section 10. This change makes the binding limits to the arbitrator's power unclear.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act outlines the grounds for vacating an arbitration award. The original clause limited the arbitrator's power, while the modified clause replaces this with a severability clause. This change obfuscates and makes ambiguous the explicit limitations on the arbitrator's authority, potentially conflicting with the FAA's intent to ensure arbitration agreements clearly define the scope of the arbitrator's powers and leading to disputes over whether the arbitrator exceeded their authority, a ground for vacating the award under 9 U.S.C. § 10.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5 . (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 ; Pub. L. 101–552, § 5 , Nov. 15, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2745 ; Pub. L. 102–354, § 5(b)(4) , Aug. 26, 1992 , 106 Stat. 946 ; Pub. L. 107–169, § 1 , May 7, 2002 , 116 Stat. 132 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 10, 43 Stat. 885 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2002—Subsec. (a)(1) to
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: U.S.C. Title 9 - ARBITRATION 9 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 9 - ARBITRATION CHAPTER 1 - GENERAL PROVISIONS Sec. 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §10. Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672; Pub. L. 101–552, §5, Nov. 15, 1990, 104 Stat. 2745; Pub. L. 102–354, §5(b)(4), Aug. 26, 1992, 106 Stat. 946; Pub. L. 107–169, §1, May 7, 2002, 116 Stat. 132.) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, §10, 43 Stat. 885. Amendments 2002 —Subsec. (a)(1) to (4). Pub. L. 107–169, §1(1)–(3), substituted “where” for “Where” and realigned margins in pars. (1) to (4), and substituted a semicolon for period at end in pars. (1
perturbation.justification: The original text limited the arbitrator's power. The changed text replaces this explicit limitation with a severability clause. This obfuscates the arbitrator's authority. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2 both state in 9 U.S.C. § 10 (a)(4) that an arbitration award can be vacated 'where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made.' By removing the explicit limit on the arbitrator's power, the changed text creates a higher risk that the arbitrator will exceed their powers, and thus contradicts the intent of the FAA as outlined in the snippets.
